mission.title: Install and Configure Kubeelasti on Kubernetes
mission.description: KubeElasti is a Kubernetes-native solution that enables scale-to-zero functionality, allowing services to scale down during periods of low or no traffic without losing requests. Installing KubeElasti helps optimize resource usage and reduce costs while maintaining service availability.

steps.title: Add the KubeElasti Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the KubeElasti Helm chart repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kubeelasti https://charts.truefoundry.com
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KubeElasti using Helm
steps.description:
Install KubeElasti in the `kubeelasti` namespace (it will be created if it doesn't exist) with the following command:
```bash
helm install elasti kubeelasti/elasti --namespace kubeelasti --create-namespace --version 0.1.21
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the KubeElasti pods are running by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeelasti
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the KubeElasti deployment. Edit the values.yaml file or use the following command:
```bash
helm upgrade elasti kubeelasti/elasti --namespace kubeelasti --set global.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set global.resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Enable monitoring (optional)
steps.description:
If you want to enable monitoring for KubeElasti, you can do so by setting the enableMonitoring parameter:
```bash
helm upgrade elasti kubeelasti/elasti --namespace kubeelasti --set global.enableMonitoring=true
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KubeElasti will show the pods running in the `kubeelasti` namespace. You can further verify that the service is operational by checking the logs or accessing the service endpoints as needed.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubeelasti https://charts.truefoundry.com
helm repo update
helm upgrade elasti kubeelasti/elasti --namespace kubeelasti --set global.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set global.resources.limits.memory=512Mi
helm upgrade elasti kubeelasti/elasti --namespace kubeelasti --set global.enableMonitoring=true

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the KubeElasti release from the `kubeelasti` namespace using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall elasti --namespace kubeelasti
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep kubeelasti | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kubeelasti
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the `kubeelasti` namespace and verify its removal with the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubeelasti
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current Helm release configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm get values elasti --namespace kubeelasti > elasti-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade KubeElasti to the latest version using the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade elasti kubeelasti/elasti --namespace kubeelasti --version 0.1.22
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the KubeElasti pods are running after the upgrade by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeelasti
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the KubeElasti pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs of the pods to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kubeelasti
``` 
Look for any errors in the logs that may indicate misconfiguration or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the Helm upgrade, ensure that you are using the correct version of the chart. Check the available versions with:
```bash
helm search repo kubeelasti/elasti --versions
``` 
Then specify the correct version in your upgrade command.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the KubeElasti service is not reachable, check the service status and endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kubeelasti
kubectl describe svc elasti --namespace kubeelasti
``` 
Ensure that the service is correctly configured and that the pods are running.

troubleshooting.title: Persistent volume claims are not bound
troubleshooting.description:
If the PVCs are not bound, check the status of the PVCs and the underlying storage class:
```bash
kubectl get pvc --namespace kubeelasti
kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name> --namespace kubeelasti
``` 
Ensure that the storage class is available and that there are sufficient resources.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
